Let's talk about how the process of co-creation activates feelings of unity. When people work together to accomplish some task or create something jointly, they feel a greater sense of weanness. Companies often struggle to get consumers to feel bonded with them and therefore loyal to their brands. But it's a battle they've been winning recently by inviting current and prospective customers to co-create with them novel or updated products and services, most often by providing the company with information about desirable features they'd like to see. The evidence is clear that this practice produces both greater favorability for the company's brand and more loyalty to it. However, within such efforts at co-creation, consumer input must be requested as advice to the company regarding how to improve its offerings, not as opinions about how to do it. Why? When you ask for another's opinion, you get a critic. But when you ask for advice, you get a partner. Research shows that these only slightly different wordings requesting advice rather than an opinion have a significant impact on consumer engagement with a brand. That's what happened when a group of online survey takers from around the U.S. were shown a description of the business plan for a new fast casual food restaurant. After reading the description, all survey participants were asked for input, but some were asked for any advice they might have regarding the business plan, where others were asked for any opinions they might have about it. Finally, they indicated how favorable they were to the plan and to the restaurant. Those asked for their advice were much more favorable and supportive than those asked for their opinions. This set of results also clenches for me the wisdom of asking for advice in face-to-face interactions with others. At work, when seeking the buy-in of others, it's tempting to ask for their opinion on your idea. Let's say for a new initiative you think would be a step forward for the organization and if adopted would bolster your workplace reputation. You know that getting your colleagues support for your plan would make it more likely to be accepted by your superiors. So taking your cue from the co-creation research, don't ask for their opinions on the idea. Instead, ask for their advice on it. That approach is more likely to create helpful collaborate. One very recent study showed that asking for another's advice rather than either opinion or even just feedback on a proposed plan produced more favorable evaluations and better suggestions for how to improve and implement the plan. I don't see how you could ask for anything more than that.
